meta.description: Structured Egyptian daily behaviors for cultural fit validation during the AHA check (Question 2). These are EXAMPLES — not an exhaustive list. Use as prompts to sharpen your Egyptian behavior answer. A product must name a specific behavior from Egyptian daily life, not a generic universal one.
meta.usage: When evaluating Question 2, ask: does this product replace or enhance any of these? A strong specific answer beats a weak general one every time.

small_space_storage.label: Small Space Storage
small_space_storage.cairo_reality: Average apartment 60–90 sqm. Families of 4–5 people. No dedicated storage rooms.
specific_behaviors: Sorting 3 children's school bags, sports bags, and cleaning supplies at the apartment entrance every morning
Stacking pots and pans in a kitchen with no overhead storage
Managing 4+ people's shoes at a single entrance with no shoe rack space
Folding and storing prayer rugs in a living room with no dedicated shelf
Managing cleaning supplies — mop, broom, vacuum — in an apartment with no utility room
Sorting children's stationery across 3 school subjects on one shared bedroom desk
Storing Ramadan decorations 11 months of the year with no attic or garage

gifting.label: Gifting Systems
gifting.cairo_reality: 15–25 mandatory gifting occasions per year. Eid visits, weddings, births, graduations, sick visits, Ramadan hospitality, new home visits.
specific_behaviors: Arriving at an Eid visit with a gift that looks presentable without wrapping paper or boxes
Giving 10–15 identical Eid gifts to extended family that look thoughtful despite the volume
Buying a wedding gift that signals generosity within a household budget
Preparing a gift for a new mother visit that doesn't require assembly or explanation
Giving a hostess gift for a Ramadan dinner that can be presented on arrival without unwrapping
Sending Eid gifts to relatives in other cities that survive transit without looking battered
Presenting a gift for a new apartment that implies quality without a premium price tag

car_culture.label: Car Organizers
car_culture.cairo_reality: 2–4 hours daily in vehicles. Car is a mobile office, storage unit, and waiting room.
specific_behaviors: Managing phone charging cables, sunglasses, car registration papers, tissues, and water bottles creating dashboard chaos
Storing children's snacks, games, and bottles for school drop-off and pickup runs
Keeping parking receipts, petrol receipts, and papers accessible without chaos in the glove box
Managing a prayer beads, air freshener, and sunscreen on the dashboard without sliding
Organizing the back seat for children during the school run — bags, water, snacks
Keeping a clean car interior visible to passengers given Egyptian hosting culture extends to vehicles

desk_study.label: Desk and Study Organization
desk_study.cairo_reality: Remote work growth. University students studying from home. Children doing homework. All sharing small multi-purpose desks.
specific_behaviors: Managing textbooks, notebooks, colored pencils, ruler sets for 3 school subjects on one shared desk
Keeping a phone charger, water bottle, and snacks accessible on a desk without taking up workspace
Organizing revision notes for a university exam when the only study space is a bedroom desk
Managing work-from-home laptop, notebook, pens, and coffee on an apartment dining table that is also the family table
Keeping a child's homework station ready every afternoon in a bedroom shared with a sibling

children_education.label: Children's Educational Tools
children_education.cairo_reality: Education is the top household spending priority. Parents invest significantly in anything that signals educational support.
specific_behaviors: Organizing a child's revision schedule across 10+ school subjects before exams
Managing educational flashcards, activity books, and learning games in a small children's room
Keeping school stationery — pens, rulers, scissors, glue — accessible but organized in a shared bedroom
Displaying a child's achievements (certificates, artwork) in a small apartment with limited wall space
Managing a child's reading books when the apartment has no dedicated reading corner

hosting_and_ramadan.label: Ramadan and Eid Hosting
hosting_and_ramadan.cairo_reality: Ramadan = highest intensity Egyptian consumer behavior. Hosting guests is a regular obligation with strong presentation expectations.
specific_behaviors: Setting the iftar table for 10–15 guests daily during Ramadan with limited counter space
Displaying Ramadan decorations — lanterns, lights, crescent — in a small apartment with no storage for 11 months
Organizing dates, nuts, and sweets for iftar presentation in a way that looks abundant and generous
Preparing Eid morning sweets and cookies for visiting guests who arrive from early morning
Managing Ramadan serving dishes, trays, and serving sets that are used once a year and stored 11 months

validation_rules.strong_answer: Names a specific behavioral moment with real Egyptian context — apartment size, traffic, hosting obligation, education investment, gifting culture
validation_rules.rejection_rule: If the behavior description could apply equally to any country — reject. The answer must be specific to Egypt's physical, social, or cultural reality.
